turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has the opinion of the Court in case 15-513, State Farm and Casualty versus United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: The False Claims Act referred to as FCA prohibits, among other things, presenting a false claim to the Federal Government. The FCA allows a private party, known as a “relator,” to bring suit on the Government's behalf. The relator relates that there has been an injury. This system benefits the relator who receives a percentage of the damage award if the suit succeeds, and it benefits the Government which receives assistance in fighting false claims. The FCA provides that a relator's complaint shall remain under seal for at least 60 days. This case involves that Seal Requirement. What consequences file if the Seal Requirement is violated? Petitioner, State Farm, issued two kinds of insurance policies that are relevant here. One, Federal Government-backed flood insurance; and two, general home owner insurance. After Hurricane Katrina, this arrangement gave State Farm the incentive to classify hurricane damage as flood-related rather than wind-related in order to limit its own exposure to claims. Respondents ar'e former claims adjusters. They allege that petitioner instructed them and other adjusters to do precisely that; to report damages caused by flood and not by wind. Now, back to the Seal Requirement. In April 2006, respondents, as relators, filed a sealed FCA complaint. The seal was lifted in part in January 2007 and lifted in full eight months later. In 2011, petitioner moved to dismiss the respondent's suit due to a seal violation. Petitioner focused on the actions of respondents' former attorney who disclosed the complaint's existence to several news outlets before the seal had been lifted in part. The district court held that FCA does not mandate automatic dismissal for a seal violation and the court declined to dismiss after balancing three factors; actual harm to the Government, severity of the violations, and evidence of bad faith. The Court of Appeals affirmed. Petitioner's main contention here is that a seal violation requires the relator's complaint to be dismissed. The FCA does not enact so harsh a rule. The Seal Requirement is mandatory, but the FCA's text says nothing about the remedy for a violation of that rule. Absent Congressional guidance, although the duty is mandatory, the sanction for breach is not the laws of all later powers to act. The FCA's structure supports this conclusion. Numerous FCA provisions do require in express terms the dismissal of a relator's action. Had Congress intended to require dismissal for a seal violation, it would have said so. This result also was consistent with the Seal Requirement's general purpose of protecting the Government's interest. Petitioner's secondary argument is that the district court did not consider the proper factors when rejecting petitioner's motion to dismiss. This Court holds that district court did not abuse its discretion. Petitioner and its amici place great emphasis on the reputational harm FCA defendants may suffer when the Seal Requirement is violated. Even if every seal violation does not mandate dismissal, however, that sanction is a possible form of relief. And remedial tools like monetary penalties or attorney discipline also remain available to punish and deter seal violators when dismissal is not appropriate. The judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit is affirmed. The Court's opinion is unanimous.
